news.title: Iraq to export crude, naphtha through Syria after Hormuz shock
news.summary:
STORY: Iraq is preparing to export crude oil and naphtha through ports in Syria, Iraqi and Syrian energy officials and refinery sources said.

:: Khor al-Zubair, Iraq

That's after the Iran war cut off its main Gulf shipping routes.

Iraq's efforts to diversify its export routes, including through Syria, will continue even after the Iran war ends and shipping returns to normal, two sources said.  

Before the war, Iraq mainly exported its fuel oil from the Gulf port of ⁠Khor al‑Zubair in Basra, southern Iraq.

Since the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Iraq has exported fuel oil through Syria's Mediterranean port of Baniyas.

Within a week, Syria plans to open two extra unloading areas and other facilities in Baniyas to handle Iraqi crude oil and naphtha, according to a Syrian energy ministry official.

Ahmad Kobbaji, deputy CEO of the Syrian Petroleum Company, said last month Syria was increasing its unloading and re-export capacity for Iraqi fuel products.

“Concerning the infrastructure, we had very little yard space in the unloading areas; the facility, pumps and discharge points - very little. So we carried out a full expansion of the yards.”

Kobbaji said Syria is also working on reviving war-damaged pipelines to replace the tanker route.

For now, tanker-truck exports are expected to begin in early July, Syrian and Iraqi officials said.

But with highways damaged by years of war, the route is fraught with risks.

This is Ahmed Saddam of the University of Basra:

"The biggest challenges may be in the dangers of land transportation, maybe in the event of the unknown, maybe some of the problems that occur on the roads leading to Syria, especially tensions and upheaval in areas along transport routes."

Under President Ahmed al-Sharaa, Syria is seeking to reintegrate into the regional and global economy.

Decades of Assad family rule and nearly 14 years of war isolated the country and devastated its economy.

news.title: Energy Sector Faces Long Recovery Despite U.S.-Iran Breakthrough
news.summary: Oil prices fell sharply after the United States and Iran unveiled a framework agreement aimed at ending their conflict and reopening the Strait of Hormuz, with markets quickly pricing in the prospect of renewed energy flows. However, industry experts caution that restoring Middle East oil and gas production, refining operations and export infrastructure to pre-war levels will be a lengthy process that could stretch from months to years.

news.title: Tech Weekly: A blockbuster IPO, a sweeping online block
news.summary:
STORY: From a record-breaking IPO to a far-reaching online ban, this is Tech Weekly. 

:: Tech Weekly

SpaceX on Thursday priced the biggest-ever U.S. initial public offering, raising a record $75 billion. 

It’s made Elon Musk’s rocket and spacecraft manufacturer one of the world’s most valuable companies.

Selling 555.56 million shares at $135 apiece, the company was valued at $1.77 trillion. 

Thursday’s pricing supports Musk's most ambitious project yet - even as some analysts question whether its lofty valuation is justified.

The company said Monday its underwriters had exercised the so-called "greenshoe" option to purchase additional shares.

Microsoft has been sued by shareholders who say they've been defrauded. 

They’ve said the tech giant inflated its stock price by failing to disclose slowing growth in its Azure cloud business.

And failing to disclose the need to spend billions of dollars on AI infrastructure. 

The proposed class action, filed Friday, was prompted by Microsoft shares falling 10% on January 29 after an earnings report.

About $357 billion of market value was erased, and Microsoft's stock suffered its biggest one-day decline in nearly six years.

Microsoft said on Monday it believes the claims are "without merit”. 

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Monday he would ban social media sites for under-16s..

And impose restrictions on gaming and livestreaming platforms for the age group. 

This amounts to some of the world's most far-reaching online restrictions, with the ban coming into place by next spring.

He said this would help protect children online while pushing back against the power of big technology companies.

"At the moment, strangers can contact any child unchecked. Just think about that. Is there a situation in the offline world where you would just let your child pair up with a stranger, an adult that you don't know anything about?”

Ford and other automakers are scrambling to obtain U.S. government authorization to continue selling certain models - despite being on the market for years. 

It’s due to a U.S. ban on Chinese-developed and -maintained software in connected vehicles. 

Ford told Reuters it’s asked the U.S. Commerce Department for authorization to continue importing its China-built Lincoln Nautilus SUV.

And said despite its tech being built in the U.S., its installation in China now requires government approval. 

Lawmakers have ​proposed making the rules even tougher.
